theorem_statement:
Glassey blow-up argument for the focusing mass-critical nonlinear Schrodinger equation.

Consider a sufficiently regular solution \(u(t,x)\) to the focusing mass-critical NLS on \(\mathbb{R}^d\):

\[
i\partial_t u+\Delta u=-|u|^{4/d}u.
\]

Assume:

- \(u_0\in H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)\),

- \(|x|u_0\in L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)\),

- the energy \(E(u_0)\) is negative, where

\[
E(u)=\frac12\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|\nabla u|^2\,dx
-\frac{1}{2+4/d}\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]

Then the maximal forward existence time \(T\) is finite and

\[
\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty
\quad\text{as }t\uparrow T.
\]

nodes.title: Finite-time blow-up from negative energy
nodes.informal_statement: For the focusing mass-critical NLS solution with \(u_0\in H^1\), \(|x|u_0\in L^2\), and \(E(u_0)<0\), there exists a finite forward time \(T\) such that \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty\) as \(t\uparrow T\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Define the variance \(V(t)=\int |x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\). By the virial identity and energy conservation, \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), so \(V\) is a strictly concave quadratic and must hit \(0\) at some finite positive time \(T\), with \(V(t)\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\). The weighted inequality applied to \(u(t)\), together with mass conservation \(\|u(t)\|_{L^2}=\|u_0\|_{L^2}=:M>0\), gives \(M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}\). Since \(\sqrt{V(t)}\to 0\), necessarily \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}\to\infty\), hence also \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}\to\infty\). Standard \(H^1\) continuation then shows the maximal forward existence time is this finite \(T\).

nodes.title: Virial identity for the variance
nodes.informal_statement:
For \(V(t)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\), one has
\[
V''(t)=16E(u(t))=16E(u_0)
=8\int |\nabla u|^2\,dx-\frac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]
In particular, \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\).
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Differentiate once:
\[
V'(t)=2\operatorname{Re}\int |x|^2u_t\overline u\,dx.
\]
Using \(u_t=i\Delta u+i|u|^{4/d}u\), the nonlinear contribution vanishes after taking \(\operatorname{Re}(i\cdot)\), and integrating by parts in the Laplacian term gives
\[
V'(t)=4\operatorname{Im}\int x\cdot \nabla u\,\overline u\,dx.
\]
Differentiate again and substitute \(u_t=i\Delta u+i|u|^{4/d}u\), \(\overline{u_t}=-i\Delta\overline u-i|u|^{4/d}\overline u\). Separating linear and nonlinear terms, a standard integration-by-parts computation yields for the linear part
\[
4\operatorname{Re}\int x\cdot \nabla\Delta u\,\overline u\,dx-4\operatorname{Re}\int x\cdot \nabla u\,\Delta\overline u\,dx=8\int |\nabla u|^2\,dx,
\]
and the nonlinear terms combine into
\[
-\frac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]
Hence
\[
V''(t)=8\int |\nabla u|^2\,dx-\frac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx=16E(u(t)).
\]
By conservation of energy, \(E(u(t))=E(u_0)\), so \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\).

nodes.title: Weighted mass-gradient inequality
nodes.informal_statement:
For sufficiently regular \(f\),
\[
\|f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}^2
\le
\frac{2}{d}\,\|x f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\,\|\nabla f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}.
\]
Applied to \(f=u(t)\), this gives
\[
M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2},
\qquad M:=\|u_0\|_{L^2}>0.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Use \(\operatorname{div}(x)=d\) and integration by parts:
\[
\|f\|_{L^2}^2=\int |f|^2\,dx=\frac1d\int (\operatorname{div}x)|f|^2\,dx=-\frac1d\int x\cdot \nabla(|f|^2)\,dx=-\frac{2}{d}\operatorname{Re}\int x\cdot \nabla f\,\overline f\,dx.
\]
Then \(|\operatorname{Re}z|\le |z|\) and Cauchy-Schwarz imply
\[
\|f\|_{L^2}^2\le \frac{2}{d}\,\|xf\|_{L^2}\,\|\nabla f\|_{L^2}.
\]
For \(f=u(t)\), mass conservation gives \(\|u(t)\|_{L^2}=\|u_0\|_{L^2}=:M\), and \(\|xu(t)\|_{L^2}^2=V(t)\), so
\[
M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}.
\]
nodes.formalization_rationale: Self-contained coercive estimate with explicit constants, minimal PDE overhead, and real inferential leverage: it is the key local inequality converting variance collapse into gradient blow-up. It is substantially higher-yield for a first Lean prototype than the broader virial computation, while avoiding the low-value elementary concavity step.

nodes.title: Negative constant second derivative forces finite vanishing of variance
nodes.informal_statement: If \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), then \(V\) is a concave quadratic with negative leading coefficient, so there exists a finite positive time \(T\) such that \(V(T)=0\); in particular \(V(t)\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Since \(V''(t)\) is a negative constant, \(V(t)\) is an explicit quadratic polynomial in \(t\) whose leading coefficient is negative. Such a function tends to \(-\infty\) as \(t\to +\infty\), so it cannot stay nonnegative for all large positive \(t\). But by definition \(V(t)=\int |x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\ge 0\). Therefore the nonnegative continuous function \(V\) must hit \(0\) at some finite positive time \(T\), and continuity gives \(V(t)\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\).

nodes.title: Vanishing variance implies gradient blow-up
nodes.informal_statement:
If \(M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}\) with \(M=\|u_0\|_{L^2}>0\), and \(V(t)\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\), then
\[
\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty,
\qquad
\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
The mass \(M\) is fixed and positive. As \(t\uparrow T\), the factor \(\sqrt{V(t)}\) tends to \(0\). Therefore the inequality
\[
M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}
\]
forces \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}\to\infty\). Since
\[
\|u(t)\|_{H^1}^2=\|u(t)\|_{L^2}^2+\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}^2
\]
and \(\|u(t)\|_{L^2}=M\) is conserved, it follows that \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}\to\infty\).

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: Finite-time blow-up uses the existence of a finite time where the variance collapses.

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The theorem concludes from the divergence of the gradient/H^1 norm as the collapse time is approached.

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The concavity argument uses the identity \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\).

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The blow-up estimate comes from the weighted inequality combined with mass conservation.

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: One needs \(V(t)\to 0\) at the finite collapse time to force \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}\to\infty\).
